rationale: The citing paper uses Kivetz, Urminsky, and Zheng (2006) in its core theoretical framing of illusory progress, not just as a background list citation. It explicitly discusses that paper's loyalty-card finding (head-start stamps accelerating effort) and then positions its own contribution as extending that line by showing in queue settings that perceived/illusory progress also raises product value judgments and spending. In the General Discussion, the authors directly contrast prior motivation-based interpretations with their value-inference account, giving the cited work a meaningful conceptual role.
relation_description: The citing paper extends Kivetz et al. (2006) by transferring the illusory progress idea from loyalty programs to queue contexts and by arguing that such progress cues affect perceived product value, not only effort or motivation. It uses the cited study as a key prior benchmark and then adds a distinct mechanism-focused contribution.
